 {"id":"git_safety_guard-6ozg","title":"[TASK] CI/CD integration for pack testing","description":"# CI/CD Integration for Pack Testing

## Scope
Ensure all pack tests run automatically on every PR and provide clear feedback.

## Deliverables

### 1. GitHub Actions Workflow Updates
Update `.github/workflows/ci.yml`:
```yaml
jobs:
  pack-tests:
    name: Pack Unit Tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run pack unit tests
        run: cargo test --package destructive_command_guard packs::
      - name: Upload test results
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: pack-test-results
          path: target/test-results/

  pack-e2e:
    name: Pack E2E Tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Run E2E test suite
        run: ./scripts/e2e_pack_tests.sh --all --json-output
      - name: Upload E2E results
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4

  pack-coverage:
    name: Pack Coverage
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Generate coverage report
        run: cargo llvm-cov --package destructive_command_guard
      - name: Check coverage threshold
        run: |
          COVERAGE=$(cargo llvm-cov --json | jq '.coverage')
          if (( $(echo "$COVERAGE < 90" | bc -l) )); then
            echo "Coverage $COVERAGE% below 90% threshold"
            exit 1
          fi
```

### 2. PR Check Requirements
Configure branch protection:
- All pack tests must pass
- Coverage must be >= 90%
- E2E tests must pass
- No new warnings from clippy

### 3. Test Result Reporting
- Generate human-readable test summary in PR comments
- Link to detailed test logs
- Highlight any new test failures

## Logging Requirements
- CI logs show: pack name, test count, pass/fail, duration
- Failed tests show: full command, expected result, actual result
- Performance metrics: time per pack, total test time

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pack tests run on every PR
- [ ] Coverage threshold enforced
- [ ] Clear failure messages in PR
- [ ] Test artifacts preserved for debugging","notes":"Update (2026-01-09):
- The repo already has `scripts/e2e_test.sh` with `--binary/--verbose/--json/--artifacts` support and a hermetic test environment.
- Prefer wiring CI to run `./scripts/e2e_test.sh` (and extending it if needed) rather than introducing parallel scripts like `e2e_pack_tests.sh`.

Implication:
- Any CI job examples in the description that reference `e2e_pack_tests.sh` should be treated as illustrative, not literal.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-6sg","title":"Design destructive pattern library structure","description":"# Design Destructive Pattern Library Structure

## Objective

Design the structure and organization of language-specific destructive patterns for heredoc/inline-script scanning.

## Background

Each supported language has its own idioms for destructive operations. We need a well-organized pattern library that:

- is maintainable and extensible
- integrates cleanly with the existing pack system
- has stable rule IDs + metadata for explainability/allowlisting
- is *false-positive resistant* by default

## Guiding principles (important)

- Prefer **high-signal** patterns that map directly to real irreversible operations.
  - Example (high signal): `exec.Command("rm", "-rf", ...)`
  - Example (low signal): `exec.Command($CMD)` (too broad; should be warn-only or out of scope)
- Pattern metadata must be first-class:
  - `pack_id`, `pattern_name` → stable rule ID
  - `severity` (ties into `git_safety_guard-1gt.3.1`)
  - short reason + optional suggestion
- Every pattern addition must include:
  - at least 1 positive fixture
  - at least 1 negative fixture targeting common false positives

## Pattern categories by language (initial)

### Python

- Filesystem: `shutil.rmtree`, `os.remove` / `os.unlink` when clearly recursive or system-path targeted
- Process execution: `os.system("rm -rf ...")`, `subprocess.*(["rm", "-rf", ...])`
- DB strings: obvious `DROP` / `TRUNCATE` when executed

### Shell/Bash

- `rm -rf` (non-temp)
- destructive `git` forms (`reset --hard`, `clean -fd`, etc.)
- destructive pipe targets (e.g., `| bash` from decoded payloads)

### JavaScript/Node

- Filesystem: `fs.rmSync(..., { recursive: true })`, `fs.rmdirSync(..., { recursive: true })`
- Process: `child_process.execSync("rm -rf ...")` or `spawnSync("rm", ["-rf", ...])`

### Ruby

- Filesystem: `FileUtils.rm_rf`, `File.delete` on high-risk paths
- Process: backticks/system/exec with direct destructive payload

### Perl

- `system("rm -rf ...")` / backticks with direct payload
- recursive delete helpers if directly invoked with a literal dangerous target

## Design considerations

### 1. Pattern specification format

Decide between:

- Hardcoded Rust (fast, type-safe)
- External files (flexible, needs loading/validation)
- Hybrid (core hardcoded + optional user extensions)

Whatever we pick must support stable IDs and deterministic ordering.

### 2. Pattern metadata

Each pattern needs:

- stable identity: `(pack_id, pattern_name)`
- language (for heredoc scanning)
- matcher form (regex vs AST query vs ast-grep pattern)
- reason + optional suggestions
- severity
- expected FP risk (notes)

### 3. Pack integration

Decide how heredoc patterns show up in packs:

- `heredoc.<language>` packs, OR
- a single `heredoc` pack with per-language submatchers

The output should still attribute a stable rule ID.

### 4. Contextual patterns

Some matchers require context to stay high-signal:

- process execution calls are only dangerous when the payload is destructive
- filesystem calls are only catastrophic when recursive/system-path targeted

This means many patterns should be designed as *composite matchers* (AST match + string/payload check).

### 5. Allowlisting

Users must be able to allowlist legitimate patterns by stable rule ID (not raw regex), with optional scoping/expiration.

## Deliverables

- Pattern specification format (schema + example)
- Initial pattern inventory per language focused on high-signal destructive operations
- Integration plan with pack system + evaluator + explain output
- Pattern authoring checklist (FP controls + tests)

## Acceptance Criteria

- Produces a clear, consistent pattern metadata schema usable by:
  - hook decision output
  - `dcg explain`
  - `dcg allowlist`
  - `dcg scan`
- Defines a policy that prevents low-signal patterns from becoming default hard denies.
- Defines the minimum test/fixture requirements for adding a pattern.
- Enables stable rule IDs that do not change across builds.

-{"id":"git_safety_guard-78a","title":"[PACK] remote.rsync - rsync Pack","description":"# rsync Remote Sync Pack - PROMOTED TO TIER 1

## Why Tier 1 (Critical)
**This pack was promoted from Tier 3 to Tier 1** because `rsync --delete` is statistically one of the most common destructive command mistakes. Analysis of developer surveys, Stack Overflow questions, and incident reports shows this is a top-5 footgun.

## Scope
Implement pattern matching for rsync operations.

## Destructive Patterns
### CRITICAL: --delete variants
- `rsync --delete` - Deletes files at dest not in source
- `rsync --delete-before` - Delete before transfer
- `rsync --delete-during` - Delete during transfer  
- `rsync --delete-after` - Delete after transfer
- `rsync --delete-excluded` - Delete excluded files too
- `rsync --del` - Short alias for --delete

### Direction Mistakes
- Wrong source/dest direction can overwrite important files
- Empty source + --delete = wipes destination

## Safe Patterns
- `rsync -n` / `rsync --dry-run` - Preview only
- `rsync --list-only` - List without transfer
- `rsync -v` - Verbose (for inspection)
- `rsync` without --delete flags - Copy only, no deletion

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: Promoted to Tier 1 (was remote.*)
- Keywords: rsync
- CRITICAL: Must catch all --delete variants including short forms

## Testing Requirements
- [ ] Unit tests in `src/packs/remote/rsync.rs`
- [ ] E2E tests in `scripts/e2e_tests/rsync.txt`
- [ ] Test all --delete variants (--delete, --del, --delete-before, etc.)
- [ ] Test --dry-run makes --delete safe
- [ ] Test edge cases (--delete in quotes, environment variables)
- [ ] Performance < 500µs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All --delete variants blocked
- [ ] --dry-run recognized as safe modifier
- [ ] Short forms (--del) caught
- [ ] Tests specifically cover delete variants

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-7ey","title":"Feature: Dependabot Configuration for Automated Dependency Updates","description":"# Feature: Dependabot Configuration

## Background

DCG has no automated dependency update system. Dependencies are updated manually, which means:
- Security patches may be missed or delayed
- New features/performance improvements not adopted
- Technical debt accumulates silently

Dependabot is GitHubs built-in dependency update tool that:
- Automatically creates PRs for outdated dependencies
- Groups related updates together
- Provides security alerts for vulnerable dependencies

## Why This Matters for DCG

DCG uses several dependencies that receive regular updates:

| Dependency | Purpose | Update Frequency |
|------------|---------|------------------|
| serde/serde_json | JSON parsing | Monthly |
| fancy-regex | Pattern matching | Quarterly |
| colored | Terminal output | Quarterly |
| memchr | Fast string search | Quarterly |

Security-critical dependencies like serde_json need timely updates when vulnerabilities are found.

## Technical Approach

### Dependabot Configuration

Create `.github/dependabot.yml`:

```yaml
version: 2
updates:
  # Cargo dependencies
  - package-ecosystem: "cargo"
    directory: "/"
    schedule:
      interval: "weekly"
      day: "monday"
    open-pull-requests-limit: 5
    commit-message:
      prefix: "deps"
    groups:
      rust-dependencies:
        patterns:
          - "*"
        exclude-patterns:
          - "serde*"  # Update serde separately (breaking changes possible)
    
  # GitHub Actions
  - package-ecosystem: "github-actions"
    directory: "/"
    schedule:
      interval: "weekly"
      day: "monday"
    open-pull-requests-limit: 3
    commit-message:
      prefix: "ci"
```

### Why These Settings

**Weekly schedule**: Frequent enough to stay current, not so frequent as to be noisy

**Monday**: Updates at start of week, time to review before Friday

**PR limits**: Prevents overwhelming with updates (5 cargo, 3 actions)

**Grouping**: Groups minor updates together, keeps serde separate (more careful review needed)

**Commit prefixes**: deps: for cargo, ci: for actions - follows conventional commits

## Implementation Tasks

1. Create dependabot.yml configuration
2. Configure PR grouping strategy
3. Set up commit message prefixes
4. Document update review process

## Success Criteria

- [ ] dependabot.yml created and valid
- [ ] Cargo dependencies monitored weekly
- [ ] GitHub Actions monitored weekly
- [ ] PR grouping configured
- [ ] First automated PRs appear

## Considerations

### Breaking Changes

Some updates may have breaking changes. Dependabot PRs should:
1. Pass CI before merging
2. Be reviewed for changelog notes
3. Test manually if significant

### Security Updates

Dependabot creates security PRs immediately (not on schedule) for vulnerable dependencies. These should be prioritized.

### PR Review Burden

5 PRs/week max is manageable. Create and maintain a small, curated corpus of high-signal command cases and a harness that runs them through:

- hook JSON path
- CLI evaluator path

## Corpus categories

- `true_positives/` (must block)
- `false_positives/` (must allow)
- `bypass_attempts/` (must block or warn)
- `edge_cases/` (must not crash/hang)

## Output/logging requirements

- Harness prints per-case:
  - description
  - expected decision
  - actual decision
  - rule id when matched

## Acceptance criteria

- Corpus harness runs in CI.
- Adding a new regression is easy and does not require writing Rust test code each time.

-{"id":"git_safety_guard-7zg","title":"Add UBS installation and run steps to CI workflow","description":"# Task: Add UBS Installation and Run Steps to CI

## What to Do

Add two steps to the check job in .github/workflows/ci.yml:
1. Install UBS using the official installer
2. Run UBS on changed Rust files

## CI Steps to Add

After the clippy step, add:

```yaml
- name: Install UBS
  run: |
    curl -fsSL "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Dicklesworthstone/ultimate_bug_scanner/master/install.sh" \
      | bash -s -- --easy-mode
    # Add UBS to PATH for this job
    echo "$HOME/.ubs" >> $GITHUB_PATH

- name: Run UBS on changed files
  if: always()
  run: |
    echo "=== UBS Static Analysis ===" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    echo "Timestamp: $(date -Iseconds)" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    echo "" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    
    # Get changed Rust files
    if [ "${{ github.event_name }}" = "pull_request" ]; then
      CHANGED=$(git diff --name-only origin/${{ github.base_ref }}...HEAD -- "*.rs" | tr "\n" " ")
      echo "Mode: Pull Request (base: ${{ github.base_ref }})" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    else
      CHANGED=$(git diff --name-only HEAD~1 -- "*.rs" | tr "\n" " ")
      echo "Mode: Push (comparing to HEAD~1)" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    fi
    
    echo "Changed files: ${CHANGED:-none}" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    echo "" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    
    if [ -n "$CHANGED" ]; then
      echo "Running UBS analysis..." | tee -a ubs-output.log
      # Run UBS with verbose output
      if ubs --verbose $CHANGED 2>&1 | tee -a ubs-output.log; then
        echo "" | tee -a ubs-output.log
        echo "UBS Result: PASSED (no issues found)" | tee -a ubs-output.log
      else
        echo "" | tee -a ubs-output.log
        echo "UBS Result: WARNINGS (issues found - non-blocking)" | tee -a ubs-output.log
        echo "::warning::UBS found potential issues in changed files"
      fi
    else
      echo "UBS Result: SKIPPED (no Rust files changed)" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    fi
    
    # Summary
    echo "" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    echo "=== End UBS Analysis ===" | tee -a ubs-output.log

- name: UBS summary
  if: always()
  run: |
    echo "## UBS Static Analysis" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "\`\`\`" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    cat ubs-output.log >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY 2>/dev/null || echo "No UBS output" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "\`\`\`" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY

- name: Upload UBS output
  if: failure()
  uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
  with:
    name: ubs-output
    path: ubs-output.log
    retention-days: 7
```

## Key Fixes from Original

1. **Binary Path**: Added `echo "$HOME/.ubs" >> $GITHUB_PATH` to make ubs available
2. **Detailed Logging**: All output goes to ubs-output.log with timestamps
3. **Clear Status**: Explicit PASSED/WARNINGS/SKIPPED messages
4. **Artifact Upload**: Save output on failure for debugging
5. **Step Summary**: UBS results appear in GitHub Actions summary

## Why --verbose

The verbose flag provides:
- File-by-file analysis progress
- Detailed issue descriptions
- Context around found issues

## Why Warning Mode

Using warning annotations instead of failing because:
- UBS is new to this repo, may have false positives
- Dont want to block PRs unexpectedly
- Can change to blocking after tuning

## Files to Modify

- .github/workflows/ci.yml (check job)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] UBS install step added with PATH setup
- [ ] UBS run step added with detailed logging
- [ ] Changed-files logic works for PRs and pushes
- [ ] ubs-output.log captures all analysis output
- [ ] Step summary shows UBS results
- [ ] Artifacts uploaded on failure
- [ ] CI passes with new steps","notes":"Testing/Validation:
- CI must capture UBS install/run logs and upload them as artifacts on failure.
- Add a small validation step that ensures the UBS command executes on a known safe file and reports zero findings (logged).","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:23:27.284229987-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-09T17:04:58.340767828-05:00"}

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-8u1","title":"[DESIGN] Architectural Decisions for Pack Implementation","description":"# Architectural Decisions for Pack Implementation

## Overview
Before implementing new packs, several architectural decisions need to be documented and agreed upon. These decisions affect all packs and should be resolved BEFORE implementation begins.

## Decisions to Document

### 1. REST API Pattern Detection Strategy
**Problem**: Many tools use REST APIs via curl/httpie. How do we detect `curl -X DELETE api.example.com/resource`?

**Options**:
A. Keyword-based: Match `curl` + detect DELETE method + known API hostnames
B. AST-based: Parse curl arguments to extract method and URL
C. Hybrid: Quick keyword check, then AST parse for suspected matches

**Considerations**:
- Performance impact of AST parsing
- False positive rate for each approach
- Maintainability of hostname lists

### 2. Pack Overlap Resolution
**Problem**: cicd.github_actions and platform.github both cover `gh` commands

**Options**:
A. Strict separation: Each pack handles distinct commands
B. Layered: Platform pack is base, cicd pack extends
C. Merged: Single github pack with all patterns

**Considerations**:
- User configuration complexity
- Pattern duplication
- Maintenance burden

### 3. Command Alias Handling
**Problem**: `kubectl` vs `k`, `docker` vs `podman`, etc.

**Options**:
A. Explicit aliases: List all known aliases per pack
B. Keyword expansion: User configures their aliases
C. Pattern-based: Use regex to catch common alias patterns

**Considerations**:
- User customization needs
- Performance of expanded keyword lists

### 4. Performance Budget Per Pack
**Problem**: Each pack adds patterns. How do we prevent slowdown?

**Proposal**:
- Maximum patterns per pack: 50
- Maximum regex complexity score: TBD
- Benchmark requirement: <1ms per pack evaluation

### 5. Safe vs Destructive Threshold
**Problem**: Some commands are context-dependent (e.g., `rsync` is safe without --delete)

**Options**:
A. Flag-aware matching: Parse flags to determine safety
B. Conservative: Block ambiguous commands
C. Configurable: User sets their preference

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Each decision documented with chosen option and rationale
- [ ] Performance benchmarks established
- [ ] Design doc created in docs/architecture/pack-design.md
- [ ] Team review completed

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-jfj","title":"Design language detection heuristics","description":"# Design: Language Detection Heuristics for Embedded Payloads (Heredoc + Inline Script)

## Objective
Define deterministic, performant heuristics to infer the language of extracted embedded payloads (heredocs / here-strings / inline `-c`/`-e` code), enabling AST-aware scanning *without* introducing false-positive flapping or expensive multi-grammar parsing by default.

This spec is self-contained and intended to directly drive `git_safety_guard-du4` (implementation).

---

## Inputs
Language detection operates on a **payload** produced by Tier 2 extraction (`git_safety_guard-o15`):

- `payload_kind`: `Heredoc | HereString | InlineArg`
- `command_context`: original command string + optional lightweight tokenization of the first line
- `payload_text`: extracted bytes (bounded) and optional `content_preview`
- `redirection_context` (best-effort): any `> file.ext` or `cat > file.ext` patterns near the heredoc operator
- `delimiter_token` (for heredocs): e.g., `EOF`, `PY`, `SQL`, etc.

---

## Output Model

Return a structured result:

- `language`: enum (e.g., `Shell`, `Python`, `JavaScript`, `TypeScript`, `Ruby`, `Perl`, `SQL`, `Unknown`)
- `confidence`: `High | Medium | Low`
- `evidence`: ordered list of `{source, detail}` for explainability

Where:
- **High** = safe to run a single AST grammar for Tier 3.
- **Medium** = run AST only if under remaining time budget; otherwise fall back.
- **Low** = do not AST-parse by default in hook mode; fail-open and record diagnostic.

---

## Deterministic Priority Order (Signals)

We must avoid “random” outcomes. Apply the first applicable rule in this exact order.

### 1) Inline-script interpreter + flag (Highest confidence)
Applicable to `InlineArg` payloads.

If the payload was extracted from:
- `python -c ...` / `python3 -c ...` → `Python` (High)
- `bash -c ...` / `sh -c ...` / `zsh -c ...` → `Shell` (High)
- `node -e ...` / `node -p ...` / `nodejs -e ...` → `JavaScript` (High)
- `ruby -e ...` → `Ruby` (High)
- `perl -e ...` → `Perl` (High)

Rationale: the user explicitly chose an interpreter.

### 2) Executing command prefix (High confidence when command is a known interpreter / REPL)
Applicable to heredoc/here-string payloads when the command that **receives stdin** is known.

We need a best-effort “receiver” detection:
- Prefer token 0 after optional `sudo`, `env VAR=...`, and leading assignments (`FOO=bar cmd ...`).
- If a simple pipeline exists and we can cheaply detect a receiver (e.g., `... | python`), we may use the **rightmost known interpreter** as receiver (Medium).

Mapping table (initial):
- `python`, `python3`, `python2` → `Python`
- `bash`, `sh`, `zsh`, `ksh` → `Shell`
- `node`, `nodejs` → `JavaScript`
- `ruby`, `irb` → `Ruby`
- `perl` → `Perl`
- `psql` → `SQL` (PostgreSQL dialect)
- `mysql` → `SQL` (MySQL dialect)
- `sqlite3` → `SQL` (SQLite dialect)
- `mongosh`, `mongo` → `JavaScript` (mongo shell scripting)
- `redis-cli` → `Redis` (redis protocol commands; treat as `Unknown` for AST, but allow a targeted matcher set)

Confidence:
- If receiver is direct command prefix: High.
- If receiver inferred via pipe: Medium.

### 3) File extension / redirection context (Medium→High)
If we have a nearby redirection target with an extension:

- `*.py` → `Python` (High)
- `*.sh` → `Shell` (High)
- `*.js` → `JavaScript` (High)
- `*.ts` → `TypeScript` (High)
- `*.rb` → `Ruby` (High)
- `*.pl` → `Perl` (High)
- `*.sql` → `SQL` (High)

If extension is found but extraction did not confirm that redirection applies to this payload, downgrade to Medium.

### 4) Shebang in payload (Medium→High)
If the payload begins with a shebang in the first 2 lines:

- `#!/usr/bin/env python*` or `#!/usr/bin/python*` → `Python` (High)
- `#!/bin/bash` / `#!/bin/sh` / `#!/usr/bin/env bash` → `Shell` (High)
- `#!/usr/bin/node` / `#!/usr/bin/env node` → `JavaScript` (High)
- `#!/usr/bin/ruby` / `#!/usr/bin/env ruby` → `Ruby` (High)
- `#!/usr/bin/perl` / `#!/usr/bin/env perl` → `Perl` (High)

If shebang conflicts with a strong receiver (Rule 2 High), prefer:
- `InlineArg` interpreter (Rule 1) always wins.
- Otherwise: if receiver is `cat`/`tee`/`printf` (non-interpreter), shebang wins.
- If receiver is an interpreter (python/bash/node), receiver wins unless shebang is also consistent.

### 5) Delimiter naming heuristics (Low→Medium)
If delimiter token strongly signals language:
- `PY`, `PYTHON` → Python
- `JS`, `NODE` → JavaScript
- `TS` → TypeScript
- `RB`, `RUBY` → Ruby
- `PL`, `PERL` → Perl
- `SQL`, `PSQL`, `POSTGRES` → SQL

This is never High confidence on its own (users often choose arbitrary delimiters).

### 6) Content heuristics (bounded prefix scan) (Low→Medium)
Scan only a small prefix:
- First `N_LINES=20` and `N_BYTES=2048` (whichever first), ignoring leading whitespace.

Heuristic signals (examples):
- Python: `^\s*(import|from)\s+`, `^\s*def\s+`, `^\s*class\s+`, `print\(`
- Shell: `^\s*(set -e|set -eux|export\s+)`, `\b(apt-get|yum|brew)\b`, `\b(curl|wget)\b` (careful: can occur in other languages)
- JS/TS: `\b(require\(|module\.exports|export\s+)`, `\bconst\b`, `\bfunction\b`, `=>`
- Ruby: `^\s*def\s+`, `^\s*end\s*$`, `\brequire\b`
- Perl: `^\s*use\s+strict;`, `^\s*my\s+\$`, `\bsub\b`
- SQL: `(?i)\b(SELECT|INSERT|UPDATE|DELETE|DROP|TRUNCATE|ALTER|CREATE)\b`

Policy:
- If exactly one language has multiple strong hits → Medium.
- If multiple languages match → Low.

### 7) Config override (Deterministic)
Allow explicit configuration to override detection:
- `default_language = "python"` for unknown payloads
- `command_language_map = { "mytool" = "python" }`
- `project_overrides` keyed by repo path prefix

Override applies at the end (or earlier if configured as “force”). The override mechanism itself must be deterministic.

---

## Ambiguity Handling + Hook Policy

### Default behavior (hook mode)
- `High`: run Tier 3 AST scan for that language.
- `Medium`: run Tier 3 AST scan **only if remaining time budget** is sufficient; else fail-open with diagnostic `language_confidence_medium_budget_exhausted`.
- `Low`/`Unknown`: **fail-open** (allow) and attach diagnostic `language_unknown` / `language_ambiguous`.

### Optional strict mode (opt-in)
A future config mode may allow:
- trying up to 2 languages (e.g., JS then TS)
- or falling back to language-agnostic high-signal patterns only

Strict mode is explicitly not default due to performance + false-positive risk.

---

## Diagnostic / Explain Fields

Return evidence chain to support `dcg explain`:
- `source=inline_flag`, `detail="python -c"`
- `source=receiver`, `detail="psql"`
- `source=shebang`, `detail="#!/usr/bin/env bash"`
- `source=extension`, `detail="/tmp/script.py"`
- `source=content_hint`, `detail="import ..."`

---

## Test Matrix (minimum)

### 10 positive identifications
1. `python -c 'print(1)'` → Python High (inline)
2. `bash -c 'rm -rf /'` → Shell High (inline)
3. `node -e 'process.exit(1)'` → JS High (inline)
4. `ruby -e 'puts 1'` → Ruby High (inline)
5. `perl -e 'print 1'` → Perl High (inline)
6. `python <<EOF\nprint(1)\nEOF` → Python High (receiver)
7. `psql <<SQL\nDROP TABLE t;\nSQL` → SQL High (receiver)
8. `cat > /tmp/x.py <<EOF\nprint(1)\nEOF` → Python High (extension)
9. `cat <<EOF\n#!/usr/bin/env bash\necho hi\nEOF` → Shell High (shebang)
10. `mongosh <<EOF\ndb.dropDatabase()\nEOF` → JS High (receiver)

### 5 ambiguous/unknown cases
A. `cat <<EOF\nrm -rf /\nEOF` → Unknown/Low (cat is not interpreter; content ambiguous) → fail-open
B. `cat <<EOF\nDROP TABLE t;\nEOF` → SQL Medium (content hint), but if multiple hints, Low
C. `cat > /tmp/x <<EOF\nprint(1)\nEOF` (no ext) → Medium via content
D. Mixed content: `cat <<EOF\nimport os\nrm -rf /\nEOF` → Low (multi-language)
E. Pipeline inferred receiver: `cat <<EOF | python\nprint(1)\nEOF` → Python Medium (pipe inference)

---

## Acceptance Criteria Checklist
- [ ] Deterministic priority order defined.
- [ ] Confidence model + hook-mode fail-open policy defined.
- [ ] Mapping table included.
- [ ] Bounded content heuristic scan defined.
- [ ] Test matrix includes ≥10 positive + ≥5 ambiguous cases.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-ksk","title":"Isomorphic perf optimization roadmap (dcg hot path)","description":"Goal: materially reduce hook latency/throughput cost while preserving EXACT allow/deny outputs for identical inputs.

Scope: hot-path evaluation (hook + evaluator + pack system + context/normalization) and targeted advanced options.

","notes":"Background: every Bash command is routed through dcg. Any added latency compounds, so we only accept optimizations that are provably behavior-preserving (isomorphic).

Principles:
- Preserve allow/deny decisions, reasons, and fail-open semantics.
- Maintain pack ordering and safe-before-destructive logic.
- Changes must be justified by measurable wins (p99 latency, throughput, or reduced worst-case).
- Prefer local, deterministic refactors before algorithmic complexity increases.

Testing and logging requirements (non-negotiable):
- Every optimization must add or update unit tests that assert exact decision parity.
- Every optimization must run the e2e regression harness with verbose logging and store the artifacts for diffing.
- Before/after perf numbers must be recorded using the same harness (with stage timing when available).

Perf investigation results (2026-01-09):
- Baseline correctness: cargo test + ./scripts/e2e_test.sh (211/211) pass.
- Process-per-command latency (release):
  - DCG_BYPASS=1 (returns after config): p50 ~1.55ms, ~643/s.
  - Normal hook allow/deny cases: p50 ~173–179ms, ~5.6–5.8/s.
  - Isolate: dcg config p50 ~1.58ms vs dcg packs --enabled p50 ~177.5ms.
- Peak RSS: hook ~15.5MB vs bypass ~4.7MB vs packs --enabled ~14MB.
- Profiling (gprofng; perf blocked): PackRegistry::new + fancy_regex::Regex::new compilation dominate CPU and allocations.

Conclusion / updated priorities:
- The dominant bottleneck is eager pack construction + eager regex compilation at startup.
- Highest-impact fix: make registry metadata-only + compile regex lazily (see git_safety_guard-64dc).
- Follow-ons after startup fix: CompiledRegex engine swap, keyword index, memoization, smallvec micro-opts.

Definition of done for the epic:
- Baseline + isomorphism test harness exists.
- E2E regression harness with detailed logs exists.
- Each optimization is validated against both harnesses and CI tests.
- Perf numbers recorded for before/after in a durable, diffable format.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:09:02.188495555-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-09T16:18:50.088924811-05:00"}

@@ -0,0 +1,125 @@
+//! Cloudflare DNS pack - protections for destructive DNS operations.
+//!
+//! Covers destructive CLI/API operations:
+//! - Wrangler DNS record deletion
+//! - Cloudflare API deletes for DNS records and zones
+//! - Terraform destroy targeting Cloudflare DNS resources
+
+use crate::packs::{DestructivePattern, Pack, SafePattern};
+use crate::{destructive_pattern, safe_pattern};
+
+/// Create the Cloudflare DNS pack.
+#[must_use]
+pub fn create_pack() -> Pack {
+    Pack {
+        id: "dns.cloudflare".to_string(),
+        name: "Cloudflare DNS",
+        description: "Protects against destructive Cloudflare DNS operations like record deletion, zone deletion, and targeted Terraform destroy.",
+        keywords: &[
+            "wrangler",
+            "cloudflare",
+            "api.cloudflare.com",
+            "dns-records",
+        ],
+        safe_patterns: create_safe_patterns(),
+        destructive_patterns: create_destructive_patterns(),
+        keyword_matcher: None,
+    }
+}
+
+fn create_safe_patterns() -> Vec<SafePattern> {
+    vec![
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "cloudflare-wrangler-dns-list",
+            r"wrangler(?:\s+--?\S+(?:\s+\S+)?)*\s+dns-records\s+list\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "cloudflare-wrangler-whoami",
+            r"wrangler(?:\s+--?\S+(?:\s+\S+)?)*\s+whoami\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "cloudflare-api-get",
+            r"curl\b.*\s-X\s*GET\b.*\bapi\.cloudflare\.com\b"
+        ),
+    ]
+}
+
+fn create_destructive_patterns() -> Vec<DestructivePattern> {
+    vec![
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "cloudflare-wrangler-dns-delete",
+            r"wrangler(?:\s+--?\S+(?:\s+\S+)?)*\s+dns-records\s+delete\b",
+            "wrangler dns-records delete removes a Cloudflare DNS record."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "cloudflare-api-delete-dns-record",
+            r"curl\b.*-X\s*DELETE\b.*\bapi\.cloudflare\.com\b[^\s]*?/dns_records/[^\s]+",
+            "curl -X DELETE against /dns_records/{id} deletes a Cloudflare DNS record."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "cloudflare-api-delete-zone",
+            r"curl\b.*-X\s*DELETE\b.*\bapi\.cloudflare\.com\b[^\s]*?/zones/[^\s]+",
+            "curl -X DELETE against /zones/{id} deletes a Cloudflare zone."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "cloudflare-terraform-destroy-record",
+            r"terraform\s+destroy\s+.*-target=(?:resource\.)?cloudflare_record\.",
+            "terraform destroy -target=cloudflare_record deletes specific DNS records."
+        ),
+    ]
+}
+
+#[cfg(test)]
+mod tests {
+    use super::*;
+    use crate::packs::test_helpers::*;
+
+    #[test]
+    fn test_pack_creation() {
+        let pack = create_pack();
+        assert_eq!(pack.id, "dns.cloudflare");
+        assert_eq!(pack.name, "Cloudflare DNS");
+        assert!(!pack.description.is_empty());
+        assert!(pack.keywords.contains(&"wrangler") || pack.keywords.contains(&"cloudflare"));
+
+        assert_patterns_compile(&pack);
+        assert_all_patterns_have_reasons(&pack);
+        assert_unique_pattern_names(&pack);
+    }
+
+    #[test]
+    fn allows_safe_commands() {
+        let pack = create_pack();
+        assert_safe_pattern_matches(&pack, "wrangler dns-records list --zone-id abc");
+        assert_safe_pattern_matches(&pack, "wrangler whoami");
+        assert_safe_pattern_matches(
+            &pack,
+            "curl -X GET https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones",
+        );
+    }
+
+    #[test]
+    fn blocks_destructive_commands() {
+        let pack = create_pack();
+        assert_blocks_with_pattern(
+            &pack,
+            "wrangler dns-records delete --zone-id abc --record-id def",
+            "cloudflare-wrangler-dns-delete",
+        );
+        assert_blocks_with_pattern(
+            &pack,
+            "curl -X DELETE https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/abc/dns_records/def",
+            "cloudflare-api-delete-dns-record",
+        );
+        assert_blocks_with_pattern(
+            &pack,
+            "curl -X DELETE https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/abc",
+            "cloudflare-api-delete-zone",
+        );
+        assert_blocks_with_pattern(
+            &pack,
+            "terraform destroy -target=cloudflare_record.main",
+            "cloudflare-terraform-destroy-record",
+        );
+    }
+}
